questions.prompt: Why does CJIS require unique user accounts on CJIS systems?
choices: To allow anonymous access in low-risk environments
To reduce the number of accounts IT has to manage
To ensure accountability and accurate auditing for each user
To make it easier to share passwords in emergencies
questions.explanation: Unique user accounts ensure each action on a CJIS system can be traced to a specific individual, which is required for proper auditing and accountability.

questions.prompt: Which is the BEST way to comply with CJIS unique account requirements?
choices: Give each user their own named account and credentials
Have a shared 'CJISUser' account and log activity manually
Let supervisors log in for all staff
Use one generic account per shift
questions.explanation: CJIS requires each user to have their own named credentials so that activity can be tied to a specific individual.

questions.prompt: Why should unused or departed user accounts be disabled quickly on CJIS systems?
choices: To reduce storage usage on the authentication server
Because it is only a convenience recommendation
To avoid confusing the help desk
To prevent former users or attackers from accessing CJIS data
questions.explanation: Active but unused accounts are a common attack path. Disabling them promptly reduces the risk of unauthorized access to CJIS data.

questions.prompt: Which action best supports CJIS account management after someone transfers or departs?
choices: Share the user’s account with a replacement to maintain continuity
Wait until the next annual audit to remove access
Disable or remove accounts as soon as access is no longer required
Keep the account active in case the user returns
questions.explanation: CJIS account management expects timely removal/disablement of access when it is no longer needed to reduce misuse and unauthorized access.

questions.prompt: What does 'least privilege' mean in the context of CJIS access?
choices: Users are given only the minimum access needed to perform their job duties
Only administrators can access CJIS systems
Access is granted based solely on rank or title
Users get access to everything so they can help each other
questions.explanation: Least privilege means each user has only the access necessary to perform their job functions, nothing more.

questions.prompt: Which practice best supports least privilege in CJIS environments?
choices: Mapping roles to job tasks and removing access that is no longer justified
Allowing users to request access informally as needed
Granting access once and never changing it
Assigning broad 'super user' roles for everyday work
questions.explanation: Least privilege is maintained by aligning permissions to actual duties and regularly reviewing and removing unnecessary access.

questions.prompt: Why is separation of duties important for sensitive CJIS functions?
choices: It ensures one person can complete tasks faster without oversight
It reduces the risk that a single person can misuse access without detection
It reduces staffing needs by combining responsibilities
It eliminates the need for audit logging
questions.explanation: Separation of duties limits the chance that one person can perform inappropriate actions without detection and strengthens accountability.

questions.prompt: Which situation best reflects proper separation of duties under CJIS principles?
choices: All users are given administrator access to speed up operations
One person performs a sensitive action while it is independently reviewed or requires dual authorization
The same person administers systems, reviews logs, and approves their own changes
Audit log review is skipped because administrators are trusted
questions.explanation: Sensitive actions should require independent oversight, dual authorization, or strong monitoring so no single person controls all critical steps.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to use automatic locking or session timeouts on CJIS workstations?
choices: To prevent unauthorized viewing or misuse when a user steps away
To avoid having to use passwords at all
questions.explanation: Automatic locking and session termination prevent walk-up access and reduce the risk of unauthorized viewing of CJI.

questions.prompt: What should you do if your CJIS workstation does not lock or time out as required?
choices: Ignore it as long as you lock the screen most of the time
Disable the screen saver so it does not interfere with work
Report it to IT so consistent timeouts can be configured
Only lock it when visitors are nearby
questions.explanation: If required lock/timeout settings are missing or broken, it should be reported so the control can be implemented consistently across systems.

questions.prompt: Why is password-only remote access not sufficient for CJIS systems?
choices: Passwords cannot be used on VPNs
Remote access is never allowed under CJIS
Compromised passwords can allow unauthorized access without additional verification
Passwords are always stored in plain text
questions.explanation: CJIS expects stronger authentication for remote access because stolen or guessed passwords can lead directly to unauthorized access.

questions.prompt: Which approach best meets CJIS expectations for remote access authentication?
choices: Multi-factor authentication on VPNs, remote desktops, and CJIS web portals
Disabling MFA prompts when users complain
Shared accounts so teams can respond faster
questions.explanation: CJIS expects MFA for remote access paths to CJIS resources, and organizations should verify there are no legacy paths bypassing MFA.

questions.prompt: Which control is most important for securing wireless access to systems that can reach CJI?
choices: Disabling logs to improve Wi-Fi performance
Open Wi-Fi with a posted password for convenience
Allowing any device to connect if it is nearby
questions.explanation: Wireless networks that can reach CJI must use strong authentication and encryption to protect data and prevent unauthorized access.

questions.prompt: Which wireless practice best reduces risk in CJIS environments?
choices: Using shared Wi-Fi credentials across the entire agency
Keeping Wi-Fi open but relying on strong passwords for CJIS apps
Restricting which SSIDs/devices can access CJIS networks and reviewing wireless logs regularly
Allowing guest devices onto the same network as CJIS systems
questions.explanation: CJIS-aligned wireless security includes restricting CJIS-reachable SSIDs/devices and monitoring configurations and logs for authorized connections.

questions.prompt: Why does CJIS restrict moving CJI to external or non-CJIS systems?
choices: External systems can’t always meet CJIS security controls
To avoid storing too much data locally
To keep files easier to organize
questions.explanation: Uploading or syncing CJI to unapproved external services can expose sensitive data to environments without CJIS protections.

questions.prompt: Which action best supports CJIS requirements when staff need to transfer or store CJI?
choices: Let each employee decide which tools are secure enough
Use any cloud service as long as it has a password
Define what is allowed, require agreements where needed, and block unapproved uploads/syncing
Email files to personal accounts for easier access later
questions.explanation: CJIS expects agencies to define and enforce allowed methods for external access/transfer, including agreements and technical controls to prevent unapproved movement of CJI.

questions.prompt: How often must CJIS security awareness training occur?
choices: At least every 2 years or when major policy changes occur
questions.explanation: CJIS requires recurring awareness training at least every two years, and when major changes are implemented.

questions.prompt: Who is required to receive CJIS awareness training?
questions.explanation: Anyone who interacts with CJI must understand the associated security responsibilities.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of role-based CJIS training?
choices: Provide the same training to everyone for simplicity
Only train administrators, since they manage access
Train staff only when a breach occurs
Tailor security training to the specific job duties and access type of each user
questions.explanation: Role-based training ensures users understand the specific CJIS requirements related to their job responsibilities.

questions.prompt: Which example best represents role-based CJIS training?
choices: Training that covers only phishing for all staff, regardless of job duties
Training that provides dispatchers, records staff, administrators, and security officers guidance specific to how they handle CJI
Training that is optional for users with limited access
Training delivered only after an incident happens
questions.explanation: Role-based training is tailored to what different roles actually do with CJI and the specific controls/procedures they must follow.

questions.prompt: Why is it important for CJIS systems to log security-relevant events like logons and configuration changes?
choices: So users can see who is logged in at the same time
To reduce system performance impact during peak hours
Because logs are required only for troubleshooting performance issues
To support auditing, incident investigations, and accountability
questions.explanation: Logging security-relevant events such as logons, failures, privilege changes, and access to sensitive CJI supports accountability, investigations, and CJIS audit requirements.

questions.prompt: Which practice BEST supports reliable CJIS audit logging?
choices: Allowing application owners to delete their own log entries
Letting each server use its own local time settings
Only logging successful logons to reduce noise
Synchronizing system clocks and retaining logs per policy
questions.explanation: Accurate timestamps and proper log retention are essential for using audit logs as trustworthy evidence during reviews or investigations.

questions.prompt: Why is simply collecting audit logs NOT enough to meet CJIS expectations?
choices: Logs only need to be configured, not actually used
Logs only matter when an incident has already been confirmed
CJIS expects agencies to regularly review and analyze audit logs
questions.explanation: CJIS 6.0 emphasizes active log review and analysis on a defined schedule, not just log collection.

questions.prompt: Which example BEST aligns with CJIS expectations for audit log review?
choices: A documented schedule, assigned owners, and recorded follow-up actions
Only reviewing logs when storage is almost full
Ad-hoc log reviews only when someone complains
Relying on users to report suspicious events without log review
questions.explanation: A documented review schedule, clear responsibility, and written evidence of follow-up actions demonstrate consistent monitoring, as CJIS expects.

questions.prompt: Why must CJIS audit logs be protected from tampering or unauthorized changes?
choices: Because log files are usually very large
To reduce the number of administrators needed
To prevent attackers or insiders from hiding suspicious activity
So developers can reuse log storage for new features
questions.explanation: If logs can be altered or erased, malicious activity may be hidden and investigations undermined. CJIS requires strong protection of audit information.

questions.prompt: Which approach BEST supports integrity of CJIS audit logs?
choices: Letting application owners purge their own records
Restricting log modification to a small security team or write-once storage
Allowing all administrators to edit logs for convenience
Storing logs on unsecured local drives
questions.explanation: Limiting access to log repositories and using centralized or write-once logging helps preserve the integrity of audit information.

questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a CJIS contingency plan?
choices: To outline how CJIS services will be restored after a disruption
To document how to improve system performance
To replace normal incident response procedures
To list all agency staff and their phone numbers
questions.explanation: Contingency planning defines how critical CJIS services will be recovered after outages such as hardware failure, cyber incidents, or facility loss.

questions.prompt: Which action BEST demonstrates that a CJIS contingency plan is effective?
choices: Only writing a plan after a major outage occurs
Keeping the plan informal and undocumented
Testing the plan through exercises and updating it based on lessons learned
Storing the plan in a shared folder but never testing it
questions.explanation: Testing the plan and updating it based on real results ensures it can actually be used during a disruption, aligning with CJIS expectations.

questions.prompt: Why must CJIS system backups be protected with strong security controls?
choices: Backups are automatically safe because they are offline
Backups are only used for performance tuning
Backups contain CJI and can be a target for attackers
questions.explanation: Backups often contain complete copies of CJI and systems, so they must be protected with access controls, encryption, and secure storage.

questions.prompt: Which practice BEST supports reliable recovery of CJIS systems and data?
choices: Backing up critical systems on a defined schedule and testing restores periodically
Assuming backups work and never testing restores
Deleting old backups as soon as new backups are created
Only backing up non-CJIS data to save time
questions.explanation: Scheduled backups plus periodic restore testing provide confidence that CJIS systems and data can be recovered when needed.

questions.prompt: What is the main benefit of formally categorizing systems that handle CJI?
choices: It helps prioritize security controls and resources based on system criticality
It determines the brand of hardware that must be used
It allows agencies to avoid documenting system inventories
It eliminates the need for separate risk assessments
questions.explanation: System categorization helps agencies understand which systems are most critical and where to focus security measures like scanning, monitoring, and logging.

questions.prompt: When should CJIS system categorizations be reviewed?
choices: Whenever systems change or new services are added
Never, once a category is assigned
questions.explanation: System categorizations should be revisited when systems or services change so that security controls remain aligned with current risk.

questions.prompt: Why are regular vulnerability scans important for CJIS systems?
choices: To replace the need for patch management
To check internet speed and bandwidth usage
To identify known weaknesses so they can be remediated
To avoid having to review audit logs
questions.explanation: Vulnerability scanning helps identify known weaknesses so that remediation can be prioritized and tracked as part of a risk-based security program.

questions.prompt: Which practice BEST aligns with CJIS expectations for vulnerability monitoring?
choices: Running unauthenticated scans only to avoid effort
Performing regular scans, using authenticated scans where feasible, and tracking remediation
Only scanning systems that are already suspected to be compromised
Scanning once, then relying on firewalls only
questions.explanation: Regular, risk-based scanning—including authenticated scans where possible—and tracking remediation progress align with CJIS 6.0 vulnerability monitoring expectations.

questions.prompt: Why should CJIS system changes go through a formal change management process?
choices: To slow down deployments and reduce innovation
To avoid having to communicate with stakeholders
Because changes are rare in CJIS environments
To evaluate the security impact and document what was changed and why
questions.explanation: Formal change control helps agencies understand the impact of changes, test them, obtain approvals, and keep records that support troubleshooting and audits.

questions.prompt: Which is the BEST example of proper change control for CJIS systems?
choices: Testing changes, obtaining approvals, and recording them in a change log
Only tracking changes when a problem is reported
Making emergency changes with no documentation
Allowing anyone to change configurations directly in production
questions.explanation: Testing, approval, and documentation of changes demonstrate good configuration management and support CJIS configuration control requirements.

questions.prompt: What is the goal of limiting installed software and services on CJIS systems?
choices: To make systems harder for users to operate
To minimize the attack surface and simplify patching
To ensure every user can install whatever tools they prefer
questions.explanation: Limiting software and services to what is necessary reduces vulnerabilities and makes patching and configuration management easier.

questions.prompt: Which practice BEST reflects the principle of 'least functionality' for CJIS systems?
choices: Allowing all staff to install any software they like
Enabling all default services from the OS and applications
Running only required services and removing unneeded applications
Keeping legacy software installed just in case it is needed
questions.explanation: Running only necessary services and applications supports CJIS configuration requirements and reduces security risk.

questions.prompt: Why is controlling physical access to CJIS areas so important?
choices: Because physical security is only a convenience feature
To prevent unauthorized individuals from reaching systems or media that hold CJI
Because physical security controls replace the need for passwords
To make buildings easier to navigate
questions.explanation: If unauthorized people can physically access CJIS systems or media, they may bypass technical controls and obtain or damage CJI.

questions.prompt: Which example BEST aligns with CJIS physical access control expectations?
choices: Using locks, badges, visitor logs, and challenging tailgaters
Letting visitors roam unescorted in CJIS areas
Allowing all staff to enter server rooms without restrictions
Leaving secure doors propped open during busy periods
questions.explanation: Locks, badges, logs, and enforcing escort or challenge procedures help ensure that only authorized persons can access CJIS areas.

questions.prompt: Why must physical media containing CJI be securely stored when not in use?
choices: To prevent unauthorized access to CJI
To reduce wear and tear on storage devices
To keep media organized for inventory
questions.explanation: Locked storage and controlled access help prevent unauthorized individuals from accessing CJI stored on physical media.

questions.prompt: Which practice BEST supports secure transport of CJI on physical media?
choices: Using locked or tamper-evident containers and maintaining custody
Emailing files instead of transporting them
Allowing anyone to carry the media if they have a badge
questions.explanation: Tamper-evident or locked containers and chain-of-custody practices help protect CJI during transport.

questions.prompt: Why does CJIS require media sanitization before reuse or disposal?
choices: Because all media must be recycled
To ensure CJI cannot be recovered
questions.explanation: Sanitization ensures that CJI cannot be retrieved once media is reused, sold, or discarded.

questions.prompt: Which method is an ACCEPTABLE way to sanitize media containing CJI?
choices: Using secure erase, degaussing, or physical destruction
Deleting files through the operating system
Reusing the media internally without wiping
questions.explanation: CJIS requires approved sanitization methods that prevent data recovery, not simple deletion or formatting.

questions.prompt: When is encryption required for CJI in transit?
choices: When CJI crosses public or untrusted networks
questions.explanation: CJIS requires strong encryption whenever CJI is transmitted over untrusted or public networks.

questions.prompt: Which technology BEST supports CJIS requirements for protecting CJI in transit?
choices: TLS or VPNs using FIPS-validated cryptography
questions.explanation: CJIS expects the use of approved cryptographic mechanisms that rely on FIPS-validated modules.

questions.prompt: Why is encryption especially important for CJI stored on mobile devices?
choices: Mobile devices are more likely to be lost or stolen
Mobile devices do not support access controls
questions.explanation: Encryption helps protect CJI if a device is lost or stolen, especially in less controlled environments.

questions.prompt: Which system is MOST likely to require encryption for CJI at rest?
choices: A laptop used in the field
A system that never stores CJI
A server in a secured data center
A printer in a locked office
questions.explanation: Mobile and high-risk assets pose greater exposure risk and should use encryption to protect CJI at rest.

questions.prompt: Why must CJIS agencies have a documented incident response process?
choices: To eliminate the need for audits
To ensure incidents are handled consistently and effectively
questions.explanation: A documented process ensures incidents are detected, reported, contained, and remediated in a timely and consistent manner.

questions.prompt: Which event should trigger CJIS incident response procedures?
choices: A suspected malware infection or unauthorized access
questions.explanation: Security incidents such as malware, misuse, or unauthorized access require immediate response under CJIS.

questions.prompt: Why does CJIS prohibit accessing CJI from personal devices?
choices: Personal devices lack required security controls and monitoring
Personal devices cost more to support
Personal devices are harder to inventory
questions.explanation: Personal devices do not meet CJIS requirements for security configuration, logging, and control.

questions.prompt: Which device is appropriate for accessing CJI?
choices: A personal phone with a strong password
Any device connected through a VPN
A personal laptop with antivirus installed
questions.explanation: Only agency-managed devices that meet CJIS technical and policy requirements may access CJI.

questions.prompt: Why must lost or stolen CJIS-capable devices be reported immediately?
choices: To allow rapid containment actions like account revocation and wiping
questions.explanation: Prompt reporting allows agencies to disable access and protect CJI before it can be misused.

questions.prompt: Which action should occur FIRST when a CJIS device is discovered missing?
choices: Wait to see if it turns up
Report the loss according to agency procedures
questions.explanation: Immediate reporting ensures containment steps begin as quickly as possible.

questions.prompt: Why is phishing a major threat to CJIS environments?
choices: It targets human behavior and trust
It exploits technical vulnerabilities in hardware
questions.explanation: Phishing attacks rely on tricking users into taking unsafe actions, making awareness critical.

questions.prompt: What should you do if you receive a suspicious email?
choices: Report it according to agency procedures
Click the link to see where it goes
questions.explanation: Reporting suspicious emails helps protect the entire organization by enabling blocking and investigation.

questions.prompt: Why does CJIS require strong, complex passwords?
choices: To eliminate the need for MFA
To make logins harder for users
To resist guessing and brute-force attacks
questions.explanation: Strong passwords help protect CJIS systems from common credential-based attacks.

questions.prompt: Which password practice BEST aligns with CJIS requirements?
choices: Writing passwords down at your desk
Creating unique passphrases for each system
questions.explanation: Unique, complex passphrases reduce the risk of compromise across multiple systems.

questions.prompt: What is shoulder surfing in a CJIS context?
choices: Remote screen monitoring by IT staff
Unauthorized viewing of CJI by someone nearby
questions.explanation: Shoulder surfing occurs when unauthorized individuals can see sensitive information on a screen.

questions.prompt: Which action BEST helps prevent shoulder surfing?
choices: Angling screens away from view and locking devices when unattended
questions.explanation: Proper screen positioning and locking devices help protect CJI from being viewed by unauthorized individuals.

questions.prompt: Why is MFA required for remote or privileged access to CJIS systems?
choices: It is only needed for administrators
It prevents most credential-theft attacks from succeeding
questions.explanation: MFA adds a second factor, which blocks many attacks even if a password is stolen.

questions.prompt: What should you do if your MFA device malfunctions?
choices: Report the issue and follow agency procedures
Use a coworker’s MFA device temporarily
Save your password in a note app for later
questions.explanation: CJIS expects MFA to be used consistently. If it fails, report it—don’t use workarounds that bypass controls.

questions.prompt: Why is storing CJI in personal notebooks or unapproved note apps prohibited?
choices: Personal notes are hard to read later
They are not secured under CJIS-approved controls
They take up too much storage space
questions.explanation: CJI must only be stored in authorized systems that meet CJIS security controls; personal notes and unapproved apps do not.

questions.prompt: Which location is an ACCEPTABLE place to store CJI?
choices: An agency-authorized CJIS system designed to store CJI
questions.explanation: CJI must remain in authorized systems with required protections, not in personal apps, documents, or notes.

questions.prompt: Why should CJI be printed only when absolutely necessary?
choices: Printed CJI is easier to physically lose or expose
Printing makes files harder to find
questions.explanation: Printed CJI requires strict physical control and can be exposed if left unattended or retrieved by the wrong person.

questions.prompt: Which printing practice BEST supports CJIS requirements?
choices: Use any nearby printer for convenience
Use authorized printers in controlled areas and retrieve pages immediately
Ask someone else to retrieve your printout
Print and pick it up whenever you have time
questions.explanation: Authorized printers in controlled areas and immediate pickup reduce the risk of exposing printed CJI.

questions.prompt: What type of encryption is required for CJI over public networks or on mobile devices?
choices: Encryption is optional if MFA is used
questions.explanation: CJIS requires strong encryption using FIPS-validated cryptographic modules for CJI in transit over untrusted networks and for high-risk storage.

questions.prompt: Which action is NOT acceptable for handling CJI?
choices: Using approved encryption for mobile storage
Using an agency-approved secure method to transfer CJI
questions.explanation: Never email or transfer CJI unencrypted. CJIS requires approved encryption methods.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of a clean-desk policy in CJIS environments?
choices: To prevent CJI from being exposed or taken when unattended
questions.explanation: Keeping work areas clear helps ensure printed documents, media, and sensitive information are secured when not actively used.

questions.prompt: Which action BEST supports a clean-desk policy?
choices: Stacking CJI paperwork neatly on the desk
Securing printed documents and removable media when not in use
Leaving printed CJI visible if you’ll be back soon
questions.explanation: CJI should be secured in locked storage or controlled areas when not actively in use.

questions.prompt: Why are unknown USB drives a major risk to CJIS systems?
choices: They often contain malware or malicious files
questions.explanation: Removable media is a common malware infection vector. Unknown drives can introduce malicious software to CJIS systems.

questions.prompt: What should you do if you find an unknown USB drive?
choices: Surrender it to IT/security for inspection
Plug it in to identify the owner
questions.explanation: Unknown devices should not be connected. Turn them over to IT/security for proper handling.

questions.prompt: Why must you verify identity before sharing CJI?
choices: Because CJIS requires need-to-know and authorized access only
Because caller ID is always accurate
questions.explanation: CJIS access is limited to authorized personnel with a need-to-know. Verification prevents accidental disclosure.

questions.prompt: Which verification method is MOST reliable before sharing CJI?
choices: Trusting the name in an email signature
Assuming internal requests are always authorized
questions.explanation: Do not rely on caller ID or display names. Follow agency procedures to confirm authorization and identity.

questions.prompt: Why is throwing CJI into regular trash a CJIS violation?
choices: CJI can be recovered by unauthorized individuals
questions.explanation: CJI must be destroyed using approved methods so it cannot be recovered or disclosed.

questions.prompt: Which is an APPROVED method for destroying printed CJI?
choices: Leaving it in a recycle bin
Putting it in a desk drawer
Shredding or other approved secure destruction methods
questions.explanation: Approved destruction methods (e.g., shredding/pulping/burning) ensure CJI cannot be reconstructed.

questions.prompt: Why should CJIS systems never be accessed over public Wi-Fi?
choices: Public Wi-Fi can expose traffic to interception and lacks required protections
Public Wi-Fi prevents MFA from working
questions.explanation: Public networks may be insecure and susceptible to interception. CJIS requires protected communication channels and secure boundaries.

questions.prompt: Which network is MOST appropriate for accessing CJIS systems remotely?
choices: Agency network or an approved secure connection (e.g., agency VPN)
Any Wi-Fi network if you use a strong password
questions.explanation: CJIS expects remote access to use secure, agency-approved methods and networks, not public or guest Wi-Fi.

questions.prompt: Why are consumer VPNs or remote-desktop tools prohibited for CJIS access?
choices: They cannot display CJIS systems properly
They may bypass agency controls like auditing and CJIS-compliant configurations
questions.explanation: CJIS-compliant remote access requires agency-controlled VPN, MFA, and auditing. Consumer tools can bypass required safeguards.

questions.prompt: Which is the BEST way to access CJIS systems remotely?
choices: Use any consumer VPN and log in normally
Use an agency-approved VPN with MFA and logging
Use screen sharing if you trust the connection
Use a personal remote desktop app
questions.explanation: Agency-approved VPN with MFA and auditing ensures remote access meets CJIS security requirements.

questions.prompt: Why is logging out of CJIS systems important, even if you close the browser or app?
choices: Logging out ensures session tokens are fully terminated
Logging out is only needed on shared computers
Closing the window always ends the session
questions.explanation: Closing an app or browser may not end an active session. Logging out ensures session tokens cannot be reused and supports auditing.

questions.prompt: Which action BEST supports CJIS session security?
choices: Leaving the session open if you step away briefly
Letting the system time out eventually
Logging out of the system when finished
questions.explanation: Explicitly logging out helps prevent unauthorized access and supports CJIS accountability requirements.

questions.prompt: Why is using personal email for CJI prohibited?
choices: Personal email lacks CJIS-required security controls and auditing
Personal email stores messages too long
Personal email is harder to use
questions.explanation: Personal email systems do not meet CJIS requirements for encryption, logging, and administrative control.

questions.prompt: Which email system is acceptable for transmitting CJI?
choices: Temporary email accounts created for convenience
Any email account with a strong password
Agency-managed email systems configured for CJIS compliance
questions.explanation: Only agency-managed email systems with appropriate CJIS security controls may be used for transmitting CJI.

questions.prompt: Why are screenshots containing CJI treated as sensitive media?
choices: Screenshots contain CJI and must follow media protection rules
questions.explanation: Screenshots are digital media containing CJI and must be protected, stored, and disposed of according to CJIS rules.

questions.prompt: Which action violates CJIS policy regarding screenshots?
choices: Sharing screenshots of CJI via messaging or personal cloud apps
Storing screenshots only in approved CJIS systems
Deleting screenshots using approved sanitization methods
questions.explanation: Sharing screenshots of CJI through unapproved channels exposes sensitive data and violates CJIS policy.

questions.prompt: Why must mobile devices accessing CJI use passcodes and screen locks?
choices: To comply with mobile app store rules
To prevent unauthorized access if the device is lost or unattended
questions.explanation: Passcodes, screen locks, and encryption help protect CJI if a mobile device is lost, stolen, or left unattended.

questions.prompt: Which control is REQUIRED for CJIS-compliant mobile devices?
questions.explanation: Remote wipe capability allows agencies to protect CJI if a device is lost or stolen, which is a CJIS requirement.

questions.prompt: Why is social engineering a serious threat to CJIS environments?
choices: It relies on tricking people rather than breaking technology
It requires physical access to systems
questions.explanation: Social engineering targets human trust and behavior, making awareness and verification critical defenses.

questions.prompt: What should you do if someone claims to be IT and asks for your credentials?
choices: Assume internal requests are always legitimate
Verify their identity using official agency channels
questions.explanation: Never share credentials without verifying identity through trusted, official procedures.

questions.prompt: Why is storing CJI in personal cloud services prohibited?
choices: Personal cloud services cannot store large files
Personal cloud services lack CJIS-required security and auditing
Personal cloud services delete files too quickly
Personal cloud services are too slow
questions.explanation: Personal cloud accounts do not meet CJIS requirements for encryption, auditing, and administrative control.

questions.prompt: Which storage location is ACCEPTABLE for CJI?
choices: Personal iCloud or Google Drive account
Any cloud service with a password
questions.explanation: CJI must only be stored in approved, agency-managed systems that meet CJIS security requirements.

questions.prompt: Why is HTTPS important when accessing CJIS systems through a browser?
choices: It encrypts data in transit and protects credentials
It ensures the website is government-owned
questions.explanation: HTTPS/TLS encrypts data in transit, helping protect credentials and CJI from interception.

questions.prompt: What should you do if a CJIS login page shows 'Not Secure'?
choices: Do not log in and report the page
Log in quickly and log out
questions.explanation: If a site is not secure, logging in could expose credentials. Report the issue immediately.

questions.prompt: Why are public USB charging ports a security risk?
choices: They can be used to install malware or extract data
questions.explanation: Compromised charging stations can be used for data theft or malware injection ('juice jacking').

questions.prompt: Which charging method is SAFEST for CJIS-capable devices?
choices: Your own charger plugged into a trusted outlet
Any charging station with a power symbol
questions.explanation: Using your own charger and a trusted power outlet reduces the risk of data compromise.

questions.prompt: Why must printed CJI be stored in locked or controlled locations?
choices: To prevent unauthorized physical access to CJI
questions.explanation: Physical protection prevents unauthorized individuals from accessing printed CJI.

questions.prompt: Which practice BEST supports secure storage of printed CJI?
choices: Locking printed CJI in cabinets or secure rooms
Leaving files on desks during the workday
Stacking files neatly in shared areas
